title: Preparing a raw fleece for spinning
subtitle: Skirting, sorting, and washing a freshly shorn fleece.
excerpt: A raw fleece off a sheep's back is matted, lanolin-heavy, and likely to contain second cuts, dung tags, and vegetable matter. Before it's useful for spinning, it needs skirting, a loose sort, and a wash. None of this is difficult, but the order matters and the water temperature for washing is specific.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, wool preparation chapter. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg, wool and sheep chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The complete wool coat shorn from a sheep in one piece.
Removing the heavily soiled, matted, or very short-fibred edges of a fleece before washing; these sections don't spin well and contain the most vegetable matter and dung.
The natural waxy grease produced by sheep skin; it coats every fibre in the raw fleece and must be washed out (scoured) before spinning.
Short fibre pieces left when the shearer makes a second pass over an area already shorn; they knot into the yarn and weaken it.

content.text: A raw
fleece
weighs between 1.5 kg and 4 kg depending on the breed and the year, and it arrives in a single shorn sheet coated in
lanolin
. The preparation stages, skirting, sorting, washing, reduce it to a manageable clean fibre ready for carding or direct spinning.
Skirting
Lay the fleece cut-side up on a clean surface or mesh table so air can flow under it.
Skirt

by pulling off the outer 10,15 cm all the way around the perimeter. This removes the edges most contaminated with dung, urine staining, and heavy vegetable matter. Pull, don't cut, cutting at this stage shortens fibres. Bag the skirtings separately; they're not worth the effort to spin but can go into compost or mulch.
Sorting
Pick out
second cuts

, short, loose fibre pieces that didn't get skirted. Pull out obvious clumps of vegetable matter: seeds, hay, bramble. You won't get everything at this stage; some comes out in washing, more in carding.
Washing (scouring)

Fill a basin or bath with the hottest tap water available (around 60°C) and add a good squirt of washing-up liquid. Do not use cold water, lanolin sets in cold and is much harder to remove. Lower 200,300 g of fleece into the water without agitating it. Felting occurs when wool is disturbed in hot water; the goal is to let the hot soapy water do the work without mechanical action.
Soak for 20 minutes. The water will turn brown with lanolin and dirt.
Lift the fleece out without squeezing or wringing. Support it from below with both hands.
Repeat in fresh hot soapy water until the water runs reasonably clear. Two to three washes is typical.
Rinse in water the same temperature as the final wash water. A cold rinse after a hot wash shocks the fibres and begins to felt them.
Drying

Press the fleece gently in a clean towel to remove excess water. Do not wring. Lay flat on a mesh drying rack or wire shelf in a warm, well-ventilated space. Turn it twice a day. A clean fleece dries in one to two days in summer. It needs to be fully dry before storing or it will mildew.
